headline: April Vegetable Prices: Farmers Warn 30-50% Hikes as Fertiliser Costs Climb
context: One-third of global fertiliser shipments transit the Strait of Hormuz. After the late-February 2026 conflict, Hormuz traffic collapsed and World Bank flagged a sharp March fertiliser price spike. Malaysia's 14-28 March festive maximum price scheme covering 27 essential items ended on 28 March.

cards.big: Vegetable farmers are warning of 30-50% price rises after Hari Raya. The Hormuz disruption is part of the story.
cards.sub: The 14-28 March 2026 festive maximum price scheme ended on 28 March. Domestic diesel, weather and supply-chain factors also feed into vegetable retail prices.

cards.big: Kpler observed 23 fertiliser-related vessels in the Gulf with Hormuz transit uncertain. World Bank flagged a sharp fertiliser price spike in March 2026.
cards.sub: The Federation of Vegetable Farmers Association said input fertiliser costs were expected to rise materially through April; specific raw-material price series vary by product.

cards.big: Lower-income households are more exposed to food price increases as a share of expenditure. DOSM expenditure shares show food's larger weight in B40 budgets.
cards.sub: Distributional harm from food shocks can be obscured in aggregate CPI. Coping behaviour (smaller portions, meal-skipping) requires separate survey evidence.

cards.big: Indonesia stockpiles fertiliser via Pupuk Indonesia; Vietnam's price law covers fertilisers; Thailand monitors fertiliser reserves.
cards.sub: Malaysia imports over 60% of mineral-fertiliser requirements. We could not find a documented Vietnam-style stabilisation list or Thailand-style public-stock reporting mechanism.

cards.big: The question is not whether external shocks are real. It is whether Malaysia has the stockpile, stabilisation framework, or farmgate-to-retail transparency to absorb them.
Hormuz raised the cost of input dependency. Malaysia lacks the buffers Indonesia, Vietnam and Thailand have built; until that changes, every external shock is felt at the wet market.
